It is observed that the best performance is achieved through an LSTM–GRU ensemble with RMSE = 2489.04 and R<sup xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">2</sup> = 0.533. The results emphasize the efficacy of hybrid neural models in capturing nonlinear dynamics in cryptocurrency markets and present future opportunities in Transformer-based forecasting models.

title: Self-Adaptive Artificial Intelligence Systems for Large-Scale Data-Driven Decision Making
authors: Md Jubayar Hossain, Mohammed Majid Bakhsh, Mohammad Abdus Sami, Md. Ishtiaque Alam, Md Mehedi Hassan Melon, Mia Md Tofayel Gonee Manik
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference for Convergence in Computing Technology (I3CTCON)
abstract: The growing magnitude, diversity, and speed of data produced across contemporary cyber-physical, enterprise, and cloud-edge systems has revealed inherent constraints with the inflexible artificial intelligence (AI) models regarding providing real-time choices. Self-adaptive artificial intelligence systems have become an attractive paradigm to overcome these challenges through enabling continuous learning, dynamic model reconfiguration and context-specific optimization in the changing data distributions and operational constraints. In this paper, a detailed self-adaptive AI architecture to serve large-scale decision making with data is provided, which involves online learning, feedback-based adjustment, and automatic policy optimization. The suggested architecture takes advantage of the hybrid intelligence methods merging deep learning, reinforcement learning, and meta-learning to reach strong performance in non-stationary and uncertain environments. Adaptive control loops are incorporated to check data drift, system performance and the consumption of resources and recalibration of a model can be done in real time without involving human intervention. The comprehensive test on large-scale datasets indicates that the proposed solution is much more accurate in making decisions, flexible and resilient to systems against the traditional non-adaptable and semi-adaptive AI models. The outcomes confirm the effectiveness of self-adaptive intelligence in aiding scalable, dependable, and explainable decision-making of dynamic data-intensive applications including smart infrastructure, autonomous systems, and intelligent business analytics.

title: Unequal Error Protection Scheme Based on Implicit Partial Product-LDPC Codes
authors: Xuemei Zhu, Jiawei Duan, Zhicheng Huang, Xiaodong Zhang, Kuan Wu, Qianfan Wang
publish: 2026 IEEE 8th International Conference on Communications, Information System and Computer Engineering (CISCE)
abstract: This paper proposes an unequal error protection (UEP) scheme based on implicit partial product-LDPC codes. All rows of the source data array are encoded by a low-density parity-check (LDPC) code, while only a prescribed subset of information columns is further protected by a Bose–Chaudhuri–Hocquenghem (BCH) code. The check information generated by the column code is not transmitted explicitly. Instead, it is superimposed on the row LDPC codewords through a free-ride coding mechanism. In this way, the selected columns, referred to as the most important data (MID), are protected by both the row code and the column code, whereas the remaining columns, referred to as the less important data (LID), are protected only by the row code. A turbo-like iterative decoding algorithm is then adopted for the proposed scheme, consisting of free-ride decoding, row decoding, column decoding, and row re-decoding. We further present an approximate performance characterization for the doubly protected MID and a genie-aided lower bound for the singly protected LID. Simulation results show that the proposed scheme provides a substantial reliability gain for the MID, while the LID performance is not sacrificed and can even outperform that of standalone LDPC coding in the medium-to-high signal-to-noise ratio (SNR) region.

title: Feature-Optimized Blood Glucose Level Prediction with Explainable AI (XAI)
authors: Aditya Das Gupta, Md. Nahin Irtiza, Shihab-Al-Rashid
publish: 2025 IEEE International Conference on Signal Processing, Information, Communication and Systems (SPICSCON)
abstract: Diabetes Mellitus is a global health problem where precise blood glucose prediction is crucial for patient well-being. Although machine learning (ML) models have shown promise, their “black-box” nature often hinders clinical use due to lack of transparency. This study offers a clear and accessible way to develop a blood glucose prediction model that is both accurate and reliable. This research introduces a step-by-step process with a comparison of different feature optimization techniques, and demonstrates that removing outliers can greatly enhance the model’s performance. The study uses a public dataset of 16,969 physiological records to train and validate the models, and shows that removing outliers can significantly improve the model’s performance. The enhanced LightGBM model achieved a Root Mean Squared Error (RMSE) of $15.57 \mathrm{mg} / \mathrm{dL}$ on the test set. Importantly, our work includes modern Explainable AI (XAI) methods, such as SHAP and LIME, to make the model’s decision process fully transparent. This work helps bridge the gap between predictive accuracy and clinical trust, offering a strong framework for creating trustworthy AI in diabetes management.

title: Content Aware Community Detection Under Simulated Attacks
authors: Yasasree Lasya Annambhotla, Asi Kuushalie, C V Sree Pranavi, Velumury Varshita, Niharika Panda
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference for Convergence in Computing Technology (I3CTCON)
abstract: The emergence of the massive social sites has led to more connectivity than ever, yet has set the stage of organized adversarial activities like the bot networks, astroturfing activists, and synthetic communities designed to influence the masses. The condition is problematic to traditional community detection algorithms, in which structural connectivity is the primary factor, since artificial nodes and edges can be used to inflate cluster boundaries. This means that there is a significant gap that is revealed by this vulnerability, most clustering methods work on clean graphs, and social graphs in reality are not clean, are noisy, and actively manipulated. The project directly responds to such robustness gap with the assistance of a multi-view fusion framework where structural, temporal and content-based information is merged into a deeper, more reliable representation of user interactions. To take robustness to a greater extent, we add trust propagation based on behavior interpretation of anomalies. Community detection is carried out by down-weighting suspicious connections by assigning trust scores to nodes and to edges, depending on the posting entropy, burstiness, semantic diversity, and bot-likelihood. It is then followed by adversarial simulation and different degrees of synthetic attacks are employed to test the system. The suggested strategy generates stable communities with aggressive manipulation,s creating greater modularity, lowering trust drift, enhancing bot isolation, and stable community assignments between attack intensities. In general, the framework provides a multi-signal defense against adversarial community manipulation, which is quantifiable.

title: Adaptive Spatial-Temporal Feature Fusion Network for Multilingual Text Recognition
authors: Praetawan Jarutan, Sirawan Phiphitphatphaisit, Zagon Bussabong, Artitayaporn Rojarath, Olarik Surinta
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference on Cybernetics and Innovations (ICCI)
abstract: Multilingual scene text recognition presents significant technical complexity due to diverse character structures, varying scripts, background clutter, and contextual dependencies among character sequences. This study proposes an Adaptive Spatial-Temporal Feature Fusion Network (ASTFF-Net) for multilingual scene text recognition. The framework incorporates a multi-layer adaptive spatialtemporal feature fusion architecture into a sequence-based text recognition, integrating a spatiotemporal feature extractor with a Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) decoder. A deep ResNet-50 backbone captures hierarchical spatial representations, which are reshaped into ordered sequences to preserve horizontal structural information. Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) layers model contextual dependencies across adjacent character regions, while a one-dimensional convolution enhances temporal consistency. An adaptive fusion mechanism combines spatial and temporal features to generate discriminative representations that encode both visual structure and contextual continuity. The end-to-end training strategy with the CTC objective supports alignment-free sequence learning without character-level annotation. Experimental evaluation on the Multi-language Video Subtitle (MLVS) and Thai-English Multi-script Text Image (MSTI) datasets show that the proposed method consistently outperforms convolutional-recurrent and attention-based baselines. The proposed ASTFF-Net achieves competitive character error rates, yielding 1.63 % on MLVS and 13.22 % on the challenging MSTI dataset, while exhibiting stable convergence and robust generalization.

title: A Secure Intrusion Detection System Against DDoS Attacks Using Machine Learning
authors: M.R.O Aswath Prakash, R Gayathri, M Braveen
publish: 2026 International Conference on Data Science, Agents and Artificial Intelligence (ICDSAAI)
abstract: The key benefit of cloud computing lies in its flexibility to adapt to diverse environments, allowing systems to scale up or down based on demand. However, this flexibility also necessitates robust protection against Distributed Denial-of-Service (DDoS) attacks to minimise downtime and its associated impacts. This study presents a hybrid machine learning framework that combines all three: K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN), Naive Bayes (NB), and Support Vector Machine (SVM), along with a Random Forest ensemble classifier, for the detection and classification of DDOS attack types. By combining a variety of algorithms within the proposed system, it successfully complements their nature to improve detection results and is more robust to evolving attack patterns, achieving higher accuracy. The framework utilises both a public dataset and a generated dataset to enhance generalisation and real-world applicability. It employs a hybrid feature selection method combining a genetic algorithm and chi-square to select the most relevant features. Integrated with ThingSpeak, an IoT analytics platform, the system enables continuous, real-time visualisation of key performance metrics. Additionally, adaptive thresholding mechanisms are incorporated to dynamically adjust detection sensitivity in response to varying traffic conditions and attack intensities. This approach demonstrates strong capability in identifying DDoS threats while maintaining scalability, making it suitable for large-scale deployment.

title: Leveraging Texture Learning Bias for Image Super-Resolution
authors: Heng Bai, Jungang Yang
publish: 2026 IEEE 8th International Conference on Communications, Information System and Computer Engineering (CISCE)
abstract: Deep learning methods have significantly advanced image super-resolution (SR) techniques, but still face challenges in texture-dense regions. Existing works focus on enhancing the ability to extract and leverage texture information, overlooking the relationships among different textures. In this work, we reveal the texture learning bias phenomenon in image SR. Through a cross-texture correspondence analysis, we harvest some unique insights, based on which we propose a method to explicitly utilize the texture learning bias for image SR. In our method, we design multiple expert modules to obtain specific texture learning bias, and design a routing module for expert selection. Experimental results demonstrate that our method can be applied to different networks to achieve promising performance in recovering different texture details.

title: Rolling Bearing Fault Diagnosis Based on RBMO-VMD and Multi-Scale Deep Attention Network
authors: Pengyuan Zou
publish: 2026 IEEE 8th International Conference on Communications, Information System and Computer Engineering (CISCE)
abstract: This paper addresses the issue that convolutional neural networks (CNNs) compromise signal temporal characteristics during feature extraction. It proposes a rolling bearing fault diagnosis method combining Red-billed Blue Magpie Optimisation Variational Modal Decomposition (RBMO-VMD) with a Multi-scale Deep Attention Network (MDANet). Firstly, the RBMO algorithm adaptively optimises key VMD parameters, effectively preventing modal aliasing and achieving precise vibration signal decomposition. Building upon this, fourteen dimensionless and dimension-dependent features are extracted to form a feature set. For fault classification, a multiscale deep attention network is designed. This architecture captures features across different temporal scales through parallel multiscale convolutional layers, while incorporating an attention mechanism to adaptively enhance key information. Combined with deep fully-connected layers and regularisation techniques, this enhances the model's generalisation capability. Experiments on the CWRU bearing dataset demonstrate that this approach achieves an average diagnostic accuracy of 99.87% for ten fault states, validating the proposed method's effectiveness and robustness in feature extraction and fault classification.

title: A Multi-Stage BERT-Based Cognitive Framework for Conversational Business Intelligence in SMEs
authors: Dipali Baviskar, Harsh Halwai, Anand Patekar, Anoushka Kachewar
publish: 2026 IEEE International Conference for Convergence in Computing Technology (I3CTCON)
abstract: Social media is becoming a more important tool for small and medium-sized businesses (SMBs) to watch competitors, evaluate customer sentiment, and identify new market opportunities. However, it is challenging to convert raw signals into understandable, actionable insights due to the unstructured, fast-moving nature of cross-platform data. A conversational business intelligence layer and a multi-platform trend analytics pipeline were established in earlier work on this project. The multi-stage family of BERT-based models that serve as that system’s cognitive foundation is the particular subject of this research. The approach uses Sentiment-BERT for polarity-sensitive refinement, NER-BERT for business-entity extraction, SBERT for semantic retrieval over trend-aware embeddings, and Intent-BERT for query categorization. Within a single pipeline, each model maintains compatibility while addressing a different reasoning layer. We explain how these models are modified for SMB use scenarios, how they work together to facilitate end-to-end conversational intelligence, and how they open up new avenues for real-time decision support, low-resource fine-tuning, and domain adaptability. This paper focuses specifically on the cognitive foundation of that system, presenting a unified multistage architecture built on BERT-based models.

title: Comprehensive Performance and Risk Profiling of ERIDS: A Resilient Ensemble-Based Intrusion Detection Framework
authors: Niyaz Hussain AMJ, Naveen Kilari, Seeniappan Kaliappan, Ramya M
publish: 2025 IEEE 1st International Conference on Recent Trends in Computing and Smart Mobility (RCSM)
abstract: Modern networks face increasingly sophisticated threats that outpace signature-based defences. This paper presents ERIDS, a resilient ensemble intrusion-detection system engineered to boost detection accuracy, reliability, and resistance to adversarial manipulation. ERIDS fuses heterogeneous base detectors classical classifiers (SVM, RF), deep temporal models (CNN, LSTM, Transformer), and reconstruction autoencoders using calibrated stacking and an out-of-fold meta-learner, and augments alerts with a structured asset-impact risk-profiling module. The Evaluation of ERIDS on CIC-IDS2017, UNSW-NB15, and proprietary zero-day traces, including tests with adversarial perturbations, measuring detection performance, latency, and resilience. ERIDS achieves F1 ≈ 0.96, AUC ≈ 0.992, latency ≈ 3.2 ms/flow, and robustness ≈ 0.89, outperforming single models and naïve ensembles. Our results indicate that ensemble diversity combined with calibrated risk scoring provides actionable decisions for real-time SOC operations, while highlighting the necessity of continuous calibration and drift monitoring. Researcher also report compute-cost trade-offs and demonstrate improved operational triage in realistic SOC deployment scenarios.

title: Performance Evaluation of Electrical Models for Bifacial PV Modules
authors: Sarah Djabali, Shamsodin Taheri, Ana-Maria Cretu, Edris Pouresmaeil
abstract: This article presents a robust and realistic evaluation of bifacial photovoltaic models. The approach mirrors standard characterization practice, enabling assessment not only of the intrinsic model accuracy but also its practical expandability under real operating conditions. The evaluation extends beyond conventional bifacial analysis and explicitly addresses rear-only performance, often neglected in the literature. The models examined are as follows: first, standard single-diode model (SDM), second, parallel-SDM, third, photocurrent-SDM, fourth, SeriesRes-SDM, and fifth, DiodeRes-SDM. Experimental measurements using Renogy RSP220DT-G1 module were conducted under different meteorological conditions, tilt angles, and surface albedo for validation. The results indicate that the DiodeRes-SDM achieves the highest bifacial accuracy [mean absolute error (MAE): 4.89%], while the SeriesRes-SDM is a comparable, simpler alternative (5.48%). Photocurrent-SDM and Parallel-SDM are most accurate for rear-only operation but perform poorly in bifacial mode. The Standard-SDM, while the simplest, introduces higher errors (7.92%). The evaluation confirms that rear operation exhibits distinct dynamics that require the development of rear-specific models. Therefore, choosing the appropriate bifacial model based on the specific application, bifacial yield prediction, system design, or installation condition optimization, is crucial for optimizing both modeling accuracy and computational efficiency.

title: CLCT: Complementary Local Consensus Transformer for Two-View Correspondence Pruning_supp1-3687024.pdf
authors: Jinhui Tang
abstract: Correspondence pruning extracts accurate correspondences (inliers) from initial ones, playing a crucial role in computer vision and robotics. This process is challenging due to the dominance of outliers and uneven correspondence distributions, which prevent fixed neighbor selection from reliably capturing local consistency and hinder the effective integration of local consistency into global modeling. To tackle these challenges, we introduce local consistency into the Transformer as a guidance signal for the first time, which allows it to adaptively perceive and enforce local matching constraints and consequently enhances global relationship modeling under uneven correspondence distributions. Motivated by this, we propose the Complementary Local Consensus Transformer (CLCT), which captures complementary local consistency by the Adaptive Feature Pruning (AFP) Graph and the Dynamic Weighted (DW) Graph and integrates them into Transformers to enhance global modeling. Specifically, the AFP graph adopts an adaptive pruning strategy to select high-quality correspondences with dynamic neighborhood adjustment, while the DW graph employs a Feature-Aware Distance block with Structural Salient Convolution to refine weighted neighborhood relationships. These graphs guide AFPTransformer and DWGTransformer, enabling a cooperative “point selection + edge weighting” mechanism that integrates local reliability with global reasoning. Experimental results demonstrate that the proposed CLCT outperforms existing methods across multiple tasks.

title: EHiJackNet: A Quantized Hierarchical Deep Learning Framework for Efficient and Robust Jackfruit Disease Diagnosis on Edge Devices
authors: V Sarvesh, S Hemanth Raja, Pranav Jeevanantham, Swarna Priya Ramu
abstract: Jackfruit cultivation is highly susceptible to various leaf and fruit diseases, which significantly impact agricultural yield and economy. Traditional manual identification of disease is labor-intensive, time-expensive, and human error may occur. While modern Deep Learning (DL) models have shown promising results and improvements in automated agricultural disease detection, standard flat-classification architectures often struggle with inter-domain confusion thereby frequently misclassifying fruit diseases as leaf textures due to background noise. Furthermore, heavy architectures like ResNet-50 are computationally expensive, limiting their deployment on resource-constrained edge devices. To address these challenges, this research proposes EHiJackNet, an Efficient Hierarchical DL framework for the accurate diagnosis of jackfruit diseases. EHiJackNet employs a two-stage "divide-and-conquer" routing mechanism built upon an optimized EfficientNetV2-S backbone. The first stage contextually routes the input image as either a leaf or a fruit, ensuring that the subsequent fine-grained disease classifiers process only relevant visual data. Evaluated on the Jackfruit AgroVision dataset containing 2,195 images across six classes, the model proposed by this research achieves a superior classification accuracy of 96.36%, exceeding state-of-the-art models including a Custom CNN (75.24%), GoogLeNet (87.33%), and ResNet-50 (92.38%). Comprehensive computational analysis reveals that while the modular three-model pipeline of EHiJackNet requires more storage space of 544 MB, it splits the computational load, processing each image with highly specialized networks to achieve an unprecedented 96.36% accuracy, effectively outperforming the flat-classification approach of ResNet-50. EHiJackNet achieves high speed by processing 64.53 images every second. We also used model quantization to lower memory usage. We used Float16 and INT8 methods to compress the system. The FLOAT16 version reduced the size of individual model components to only 38.52 megabytes. These updates make the framework highly efficient for on-device inference in resource-constrained rural environments.

title: Effects of Ball Bearing Structure and Material: Parasitic Capacitance, Shaft Voltage, and Electrostatic Discharge
authors: Byoung-Wook Jo, Jun-Hyeok Heo, Jin Hur
abstract: Bearings are essential mechanical components that support the rotating shaft and reduce friction, thereby enabling the stable operation of mechanical systems. However, bearings are simultaneously subjected not only to mechanical loads but also to electrical stress induced by shaft voltage. Because the clearance between conductive components such as the inner race, outer race, and balls is extremely small, electrostatic discharge can occur even at low shaft voltages. Such discharge phenomena lead to electrical discharge machining (EDM), which accelerates wear and surface damage on the inner race, outer race, and balls, ultimately resulting in bearing failure. To mitigate electrical corrosion, ceramic ball bearings have recently been adopted for the drive-side bearings in traction motors for electric vehicles (EVs). Also, due to the extremely small clearances between the inner race, outer race, and balls, bearings inherently exhibit relatively high parasitic capacitance compared to their size, which significantly influences shaft voltage characteristics. Nevertheless, research focusing on shaft voltage behavior associated with bearings remains limited. Therefore, this study investigates the parasitic capacitance, shaft voltage, and electrostatic discharge characteristics of ball bearings as functions of bearing structure and material. Furthermore, the analytical results are experimentally validated.

title: Front Cover
authors: N/A

title: Event-Triggered MPC With Linear Inter-Event Control for AV Path Tracking
authors: Zhaodong Zhou, Jun Chen
abstract: Model predictive control (MPC) is widely used for autonomous vehicle path tracking due to its ability to handle system constraints and optimize performance over a prediction horizon. However, frequent online optimization imposes high computational demands, making the real-time implementation of MPC challenging. Event-triggered MPC aims to solve this issue by updating control actions only when a predefined condition is met, but it executes precomputed control sequences in an open-loop fashion between events, potentially allowing errors to accumulate. This paper proposes an event-triggered MPC framework integrated with a linear inter-event control mechanism to address this limitation. The proposed inter-event controller applies a least-squares-based linear model to generate control inputs in real-time during inter-event periods, enabling continuous feedback corrections. Experimental results on a Quanser QCar2 platform demonstrate that the proposed approach improves tracking accuracy by 10% while significantly reducing the number of MPC optimizations compared to standard event-triggered MPC, offering an efficient solution for real-time path tracking problem.

title: RAW-PM: Reliability-Aware Weighted Polar Mapping for Hard-Decision Decoding in 3-D NAND Flash Memory
authors: Debao Wei, Huqi Xiang, Dixin Ma, Yongchao Wang, Liyan Qiao
publish: IEEE Transactions on Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) Systems
abstract: As 3-D <sc xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">nand</small> flash memory continues to scale toward higher density, significant reliability disparities arise among different pages in triple-level cell (TLC) flash, posing severe challenges to error-correction codes (ECCs) in the middle and late stages of flash lifetime. Polar codes have been introduced into flash storage systems due to their near-capacity performance; however, their decoding heavily relies on the accuracy of log-likelihood ratios (LLRs). Hard-decision sensing in flash memory leads to severe loss of soft information, thereby limiting the achievable error-correction performance. To address these challenges, this article proposes a page-aware reliability optimization scheme for polar codes in 3-D TLC flash memory. First, a page-aware polar bit mapping (PA-PBM) scheme is proposed, which exploits the mismatch between polar bit importance and page reliability by mapping critical bits to highly reliable pages and frozen bits to less reliable pages, thereby reducing the probability of critical errors. Building upon PA-PBM, a reliability-aware weighted polar mapping (RAW-PM) scheme is further developed, which incorporates page-level raw bit error rate (RBER) information to construct confidence-differentiated pseudo-soft LLRs and refines the bit mapping strategy, enabling weighted path metric (PM) correction and improving decoding accuracy under hard-decision sensing. Extensive experiments are conducted on a flash memory test platform to evaluate the proposed schemes. The results demonstrate that RAW-PM significantly improves the frame error-correction success rate in the middle and late stages of flash lifetime and reduces the post-ECC uncorrectable bit error rate (UBER) by more than one order of magnitude. Under typical large-scale access, RAW-PM introduces no additional read operations, requires no changes to the decoder architecture, and achieves these gains with minimal control and computational overhead, demonstrating excellent practical feasibility.

title: Gait-Driven Pneumatic Pressure Accumulation Utilizing Multi-Stage Compression
authors: Taiki Yamaguchi, Kazuki Higashi, Kensuke Harada
abstract: This paper proposes a pneumatic generation mechanism that enables compact and self-contained air compression based on a multi-stage accumulation process. The proposed method employs a serially connected multi-cylinder configuration, in which each stage operates with a deliberate phase difference to efficiently accumulate compressed air within a limited volume. Furthermore, by incorporating an intermittent knock-cam mechanism, the multi-stage compression process can be mechanically driven by periodic external forces such as human gait. In this study, the proposed mechanism was implemented as a shoe-mounted device, where the knock action is switched at every step, thereby realizing stepwise pressure accumulation through walking. This structure enables high-pressure air generation up to approximately 1 MPa in a fully mechanical manner without relying on electrical or chemical energy sources. Both simulation and experimental results demonstrate that the proposed mechanism achieves stepwise compression and functions as a compact, high-pressure, and power-free pneumatic source that has been difficult to realize with existing wearable pneumatic generation approaches.

title: A Convergent Continuous Contact Solver With Explicit Separation Time for High-Stiffness Contact
authors: Tianjian Lei, Junpeng Chen, Qifei Li, Jian S. Dai, Yang Pan
abstract: Most modern contact solvers in multibody system (MBS) simulators adopt optimization-based relaxations that introduce a finite contact stiffness to enable smooth, invertible dynamics. While robust at moderate stiffness, these methods tend to diverge under the high stiffness required for realistic rigid-body contact, forcing artificial softening that compromises physical fidelity. This article presents a convergent continuous contact solver with explicit separation time, designed to support realistic high-stiffness contact dynamics. The solver derives analytical penetration dynamics under continuous contact, from which the contact separation time is explicitly determined. This enables robust handling of contact interactions with theoretically unbounded contact stiffness. The method is implemented in an open-source MBS simulator and evaluated on bouncing-object benchmarks and quadruped robot locomotion tasks. Experimental results demonstrate convergent and physically consistent simulations at contact stiffness values up to <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"><tex-math notation="LaTeX">$10^{30}\,\mathrm{N/m}$</tex-math></inline-formula>, showing that the solver handles both low- and high-stiffness contact within a single implementation.

title: IEEE Foundation
authors: N/A

title: Mathematical Framework for Harmonic Analysis of Cluster Stream Buffer Switching in MMC HVDC: Chebyshev-Based Approach and Insulation
authors: Chan-Ki Kim
abstract: This paper presents a novel analytical framework for harmonic characterization of the Cluster Stream Buffer (CSB) switching method in Modular Multilevel Converter (MMC)-based Voltage Source Converters (VSCs). Due to the nonlinear nature of Nearest Level Control (NLC), conventional Fourier-based approaches are not well suited for harmonic analysis of CSB. To address this limitation, a Chebyshev polynomial-based method is proposed, enabling a closed-form representation of harmonic components as functions of the modulation index and switching level. The proposed approach provides improved analytical insight and computational efficiency compared to conventional methods. Furthermore, a design methodology integrating harmonic analysis with dielectric stress evaluation is introduced for optimal switching level selection. The results show that a minimum of 33 levels is required under typical conditions, while higher levels (e.g., 49 levels) may be necessary under increased harmonic stress.

title: Image Dehazing of Unmanned Aerial Vehicles Based on Physics-Guided Diffusion Model
authors: Yuxin Zhu, Jun Li, Qinghong Sheng, Xiao Ling, Bo Wang, Fan Gao, Piji Li
publish: IEEE Journal of Selected Topics in Applied Earth Observations and Remote Sensing
abstract: Drone vision is often degraded by cloud occlusion, which causes blurring or loss of surface observation information and significantly hinders subsequent image processing and interpretation tasks. Notably, fine details of ground objects can sometimes remain partially visible beneath thin clouds. Meanwhile, recent advances in diffusion models have demonstrated strong generative capabilities, making them promising candidates for addressing thin cloud occlusion. In this paper, we propose a physics-guided image dehazing framework for drone vision based on diffusion model. The framework operates in two stages. In the first stage, a multi-scale non-local U-Net, guided by the atmospheric scattering model, is designed to estimate physical parameters such as the transmittance map and global atmospheric light. In the second stage, the strong generative capacity of the diffusion model is integrated with the physical priors obtained in the first stage, endowing the diffusion process with physical awareness to better compensate for information loss caused by clouds.Extensive experiments on both synthetic and real-world datasets demonstrate that the proposed method achieves competitive performance compared to state-of-the-art approaches.

title: SmartData Interlock: An Access Control Architecture and Data Exchange Model for Secure Data Ecosystem in Smart Buildings
authors: Ravi Sharma, Avinash Ramachandruni, Ekaterina Petrova, Pieter Pauwels
abstract: Smart buildings increasingly rely on heterogeneous data sources, including real-time Internet of Things (IoT) streams, point cloud scans, and Building Information Modelling (BIM), to optimise energy consumption, safety, and operational efficiency. However, the integration of these data sources introduces significant challenges, particularly in terms of ensuring user privacy and data sovereignty across distributed systems. Building operators, tenants, service providers, and emergency responders often require access to overlapping datasets with different legal, organisational, and contextual constraints. Without appropriate governance mechanisms, data sharing risks violating privacy regulations and exposing sensitive information. To address these challenges, this paper first discusses the development of Personalised Data Hubs (PDHs) based on the International Data Spaces (IDS) concept, which integrates multiple data sets into a unified, compliant framework. Building on PDHs, we present the SmartData Interlock (SDI) model, a graph-based, sovereignty-aware access control and data exchange architecture that enables controlled data sharing, allows users to define their own access policies, and ensures regulatory compliance. SDI extends Category Based Data Access (CBDA) models with purpose binding, delegation semantics, contextual constraint enforcement, and executable policy graphs for lifecycle-aware smart building governance. Using a realistic smart building policy graph, this work evaluates SDI performance and shows that secure data exchange can be achieved with bounded computational overhead, confirming its practical viability in smart building applications and urban planning.

title: Influence of Mode-Induced Strain Gradients on Magnetization Reversal and Frequency Sensitivity in Magnetoelectric MEMS Resonators
authors: Yuxi Wang, Daozheng Luo, Mingye Du, Jiawei Li, Tao Wu
abstract: Magnetoelectric MEMS resonators based on the ΔE effect enable frequency-based magnetic sensing through magnetic-fieldinduced modulation of the effective elastic modulus. In suspended resonators, operating-mode-induced strain gradients influence magnetization reversal and ΔE-driven frequency modulation, but their roles remain insufficiently explored. Here, we demonstrate a structural approach to mitigate such strain gradients by introducing a Pt interfacial layer in suspended ΔE magnetoelectric resonators. Finite-element analysis and micromagnetic simulations show that the FeGaB/Pt bilayer reduces the strain gradient and promotes more coherent magnetization reversal. Magnetic-field-dependent measurements reveal an enhanced maximum frequency shift and a more than fivefold improvement in peak field sensitivity, increasing from ∼ 11 to ∼ 56 kHz/mT. These results establish strain-gradient engineering as an effective strategy for optimizing on-chip magnetoelectric resonators.

title:
Broadband and High-Purity Circular TE
                    <sub>01</sub>
                    Mode Converter Enabled by an Eight-Aperture Coupling Topology
authors: Qinghe Zhuang, Chengwei Yuan, Lulu Jin, Yunfei Sun, Feng Yan, Hui Ning
publish: IEEE Transactions on Microwave Theory and Techniques
abstract: A novel topological structure and design methodology for a rectangular fundamental TE<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">10</sub> to circular TE<sub xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink">01</sub> mode converter is presented in this article. The transmission performance is substantially improved through three key innovations: 1) the implementation of an eight-aperture sidewall coupling structure effectively suppresses higher-order parasitic modes; 2) the introduction of a composite impedance-matching scheme significantly broadens the operating bandwidth; and 3) the integration of an eight-way stacked power division network further enhances structural compactness. The analysis of the mode conversion principle, numerical simulations, fabrication, and experimental measurement of the X-band prototypes are described. Back-to-back measurement results exhibited good agreement with simulation prediction. Measurement results indicate that within the frequency range of 7.9–12.2 GHz (relative bandwidth of 42.8%), the converter exhibits an insertion loss less than 0.35 dB, a reflection coefficient lower than −16 dB, and a mode purity higher than 99.3%. The overall longitudinal length is only <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$4.5\lambda _{0}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> (where <inline-formula xmlns:mml="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML" xmlns:xlink="http://www.w3.org/1999/xlink"> <tex-math notation="LaTeX">$\lambda _{0}$</tex-math> </inline-formula> is the wavelength at 10 GHz). This converter supports modular layered precision manufacturing and demonstrates clear advantages in broadband performance, high conversion efficiency, and structural compactness.

title: Meta-Memory for Large Language Models
authors: Bin Liang, Cai Ke, Runcong Zhao, Qinglin Zhu, Lin Gui, Yue Yu, Hui Wang, Ruifeng Xu, Kam-Fai Wong
publish: IEEE Transactions on Audio, Speech and Language Processing
abstract: Integrating memory components into large language models (LLMs) can improve the generation quality for long-term conversations. However, existing memory management methods largely overlook the cognition and regulation of the memory process, lacking the capability to dynamically manage and utilize memory on demand. To address this challenge, this paper approaches Meta-Memory for Memory Management (M$^{4}$), a novel paradigm that equips LLMs with the ability for self-monitoring and self-reflective memory management. In long-term conversations, where dialogue history accumulates continuously, the meta-memory capability of M$^{4}$ enables LLMs to autonomously 1) identify what knowledge needs to be memorized; 2) determine how to construct and store memory; 3) monitor the correctness and validity of the acquired information; and 4) decide when to learn more and how to retrieve information to refine their responses. Experimental results on two long-term conversation datasets and two long-term question-answering datasets demonstrate that our M$^{4}$ significantly enhances the memory management capacity of LLMs in long-term information learning, achieving more efficient storage and higher-quality response generation.

title: SHARE-U: Semantic-aware Human Avatar Reconstruction with Efficient Gaussians via Uncertainty-weighted Contrastive Learning_supp1-3685541.pdf
authors: Zheheng Jiang
abstract: We present SHARE-U, a 3D Gaussian Splatting (3DGS)-based human model, represented by significantly fewer Gaussians, achieving high-fidelity quality with fast training and real-time rendering speed. To constrain 3D Gaussian optimization to adapt it for monocular human modeling, regularization-based models rely on rigid rules, while another trend is employing more flexible contrastive learning. However, existing methods typically require a large number of Gaussians to represent a human avatar, increasing memory consumption and reducing rendering efficiency, and struggle to achieve photo-realistic rendering with fewer Gaussians. This limitation stems from their uniform 3D constraints to all Gaussians, which fail to distinguish between well-observed and poorly-observed Gaussians, leading to over-constraints on poorly-observed ones with large reconstruction errors and thereby degrading local details. To address these challenges, SHARE-U relies on uncertainty-weighted contrastive learning and semantic-aware optimization. Specifically, SHARE-U applies Hessian-derived uncertainty to distinguish and weight each Gaussian sample in contrastive learning, strengthening supervision for low-uncertainty Gaussians and attenuating the influence of high-uncertainty ones. During optimization, we innovatively propose a semantic-aware densification strategy to adaptively control Gaussians' density according to different body parts' granularity. For modeling finer textures without increasing the Gaussian number, we further suggest a Fourier loss to align texture within each semantic part in the frequency domain. Therefore, this cohesive framework forms the key insight of SHARE-U: the semantic-aware optimization yields an efficient but under-constrained Gaussian representation, and the uncertainty-weighted contrastive learning specifically enables its robust optimization. Extensive experiments on the ZJU_Mocap and MonoCap datasets demonstrate that SHARE-U outperforms previous state-of-the-art human models both quantitatively and qualitatively.

title: Monolithically-Integrated Inkjet-Printed Magneto-Electric Dipole Antennas and Arrays
authors: Kevin Martin, Dimitra Psychogiou
publish: IEEE Transactions on Antennas and Propagation
abstract: This article reports on a new class of fully monolithically-integrated 3D antennas and antenna arrays based on single-polarized and dual-polarized magneto-electric (ME) dipole elements. A new multi-material piezo drop-on-demand inkjet printing integration scheme is used to facilitate monolithic 3D integration, size compactness and performance enhancement. For proof-of-concept validation purposes, prototypes of single-and dual-polarized antenna elements and arrays were designed, manufactured, and tested at Ku-band. They include: i) a single-polarized magneto-electric (SPME) dipole antenna with center frequency (fcen) of 13 GHz, gain of 6.3 dB and bandwidth (BW) of 4.2 GHz, ii) a dual-polarized ME (DPME) dipole antenna with fcen of 15 GHz, gain of 5.8 dB and overlapped BW of 3.5 GHz, iii) a 1×4 SPME-based antenna array with fcen of 13 GHz, gain of 7.5 dB and BW of 4.61 GHz, and iv) a 2×2 DPME-based antenna array with fcen of 15 GHz, gain of 8.36 dB and overlapped BW of 3.85 GHz.

authors: N/A

title: A UB-ETDA Based Spaceborne SAR Parameter Design Method for Enhanced Extended Target Detection Performance
authors: Huaping Xu, Chonghang Wang, Jingwen Li, Bing Sun, Wei Liu, Xianghua Liu
publish: IEEE Transactions on Geoscience and Remote Sensing
abstract: For extended target detection, most existing space-borne SAR parameter design methods primarily focus on ensuring normal system operation, with limited consideration of the impact of SAR parameters on extended target detection performance. To enhance the extended target detection performance of spaceborne SAR from a parameter design perspective, a novel parameter design method based on an upper bound on extended target detection accuracy (UB-ETDA) is proposed, which comprehensively accounts for key factors affecting extended target detection performance, including aspect-dependent scattering characteristics of the extended target and spatial correlation properties of clutter. By employing UB-ETDA as a new performance metric to guide spaceborne SAR parameter design, the extended target detection performance of the system can be effectively improved. First, a statistical signal model for spaceborne SAR extended target detection is developed, systematically characterizing the stochastic properties of the extended target, background clutter and noise components in SAR images. Then, the expression of UB-ETDA for SAR system is rigorously derived, along with a stable and statistically efficient numerical computation method. Finally, with UB-ETDA as the primary performance metric and existing performance metrics as constraints, a spaceborne SAR parameter design method is proposed for improving extended target detection performance. Simulation results validate the effectiveness of the proposed method.

title: Classifying ADHD Presentations Using Temporally Segmented Behavioral Data from a Serious Game
authors: Seung-Jae Kim, Jun-Su Kim, Unhui Jo, Yoo Joo Jeong, Su Jin Jun, Jin-Yeop Park, Hyang-Sook Hoe, Jeong-Heon Song
publish: IEEE Journal of Biomedical and Health Informatics
abstract: Distinguishing attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) presentations, such as predominantly inattentive (ADHD-I) and hyperactive/impulsive (ADHD-HI), is clinically important for management. However, traditional ADHD assessments often rely on subjective evaluations, potentially introducing biases and limiting diagnostic accuracy. As a more objective approach, this study aimed to develop a machine learning-based classification model using behavioral data derived from serious games performed by children diagnosed with ADHD. A total of 51 children aged 6 to 13 participated, and both parent-reported Korean-ADHD Rating Scale scores and serious game performance data were collected. Behavioral data collected from serious games were temporally segmented into early and late phases of gameplay, and discriminative behavioral features were identified through a machine learning-based feature selection process. Three machine learning models, Random Forest, Support Vector Machine, and eXtreme Gradient Boosting, were trained and evaluated. Models using temporally segmented behavioral features showed strong classification performance, particularly for the ensemble-based models. Among them, the Random Forest model demonstrated the best test performance, achieving an accuracy of 81.818%, an F1-score of 85.714%, and an AUROC of 83.333%. Furthermore, behavioral differences between the ADHD-I and ADHD-HI groups became more pronounced during the latter half of gameplay. In particular, children with ADHD-I showed longer decision times, longer times to the final touch, more unnecessary touches, and lower maximum difficulty levels than those with ADHD-HI, indicating that cognitive processing becomes slower and response strategies become less efficient as gameplay progresses into the later phase. These findings indicate that models using serious game-derived behavioral data, particularly with temporal feature engineering, can effectively classify ADHD presentations and may serve as a scalable, quantitative tool to support clinical assessment and the development of personalized interventions.
